input.description: Explore plugin SDK and manifest patterns
input.prompt:
I need to understand the plugin manifest format and plugin SDK contracts for channel plugins in the OpenClaw codebase.

Please read these specific files in full:
1. src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts
2. src/plugin-sdk/core.ts (or the relevant parts about channel plugins)
3. src/plugins/types.ts (plugin manifest types)
4. Any openclaw.plugin.json files you can find in bundled channel plugins (IRC, Nostr, Matrix, Telegram, Zalo, etc.)
5. docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md if it exists
6. docs/plugins/manifest.md if it exists
7. src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts
8. src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts

Also look at the bundled plugin tree - find its root AGENTS.md to understand the plugin structure conventions.

Report the full content of each file (or substantial excerpts if very long). I need this to understand what interface a new channel plugin must implement.

input.description: Explore complete IRC or Nostr plugin implementation
input.prompt:
I need to read a complete channel plugin implementation in the OpenClaw codebase to use as a template for a new plugin. 

Please:
1. Find the IRC plugin (search for "irc" in workspace package directories, plugin directories, etc.)
2. If IRC exists, read ALL of its source files completely
3. Also find the Nostr plugin and read all of its source files
4. Look for any simpler/smaller channel plugins that would be good templates

For each plugin found, I need:
- Full directory listing
- Full content of package.json
- Full content of openclaw.plugin.json  
- Full content of all TypeScript source files (src/*.ts)
- Full content of any test files

I'm trying to implement a new "PulseBoard" channel plugin following the exact same patterns, so I need to understand every file that needs to be created.

1 --- 2 title: "Building Channel Plugins" 3 sidebarTitle: "Channel Plugins" 4 summary: "Step-by-step guide to building a messaging channel plugin for OpenClaw" 5 read_when: 6 - You are building a new messaging channel plugin 7 - You want to connect OpenClaw to a messaging platform 8 - You need to understand the ChannelPlugin adapter surface 9 --- 10 11 # Building Channel Plugins 12 13 This guide walks through building a channel plugin that connects OpenClaw to a 14 messaging platform. By the end you will have a working channel with DM security, 15 pairing, reply threading, and outbound messaging. 16 17 18 If you have not built any OpenClaw plugin before, read 19 [Getting Started](/plugins/building-plugins) first for the basic package 20 structure and manifest setup. 21 22 23 ## How channel plugins work 24 25 Channel plugins do not need their own send/edit/react tools. OpenClaw keeps one 26 shared `message` tool in core. Your plugin owns: 27 28 - **Config** — account resolution and setup wizard 29 - **Security** — DM policy and allowlists 30 - **Pairing** — DM approval flow 31 - **Session grammar** — how provider-specific conversation ids map to base chats, thread ids, and parent fallbacks 32 - **Outbound** — sending text, media, and polls to the platform 33 - **Threading** — how replies are threaded 34 35 Core owns the shared message tool, prompt wiring, the outer session-key shape, 36 generic `:thread:` bookkeeping, and dispatch. 37 38 If your platform stores extra scope inside conversation ids, keep that parsing 39 in the plugin with `messaging.resolveSessionConversation(...)`. That is the 40 canonical hook for mapping `rawId` to the base conversation id, optional thread 41 id, explicit `baseConversationId`, and any `parentConversationCandidates`. 42 When you return `parentConversationCandidates`, keep them ordered from the 43 narrowest parent to the broadest/base conversation. 44 45 Bundled plugins that need the same parsing before the channel registry boots 46 can also expose a top-level `session-key-api.ts` file with a matching 47 `resolveSessionConversation(...)` export. Core uses that bootstrap-safe surface 48 only when the runtime plugin registry is not available yet. 49 50 `messaging.resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` remains available as a 51 legacy compatibility fallback when a plugin only needs parent fallbacks on top 52 of the generic/raw id. If both hooks exist, core uses 53 `resolveSessionConversation(...).parentConversationCandidates` first and only 54 falls back to `resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` when the canonical hook 55 omits them. 56 57 ## Approvals and channel capabilities 58 59 Most channel plugins do not need approval-specific code. 60 61 - Core owns same-chat `/approve`, shared approval button payloads, and generic fallback delivery. 62 - Prefer one `approvalCapability` object on the channel plugin when the channel needs approval-specific behavior. 63 - `approvalCapability.authorizeActorAction` and `approvalCapability.getActionAvailabilityState` are the canonical approval-auth seam. 64 - Use `outbound.shouldSuppressLocalPayloadPrompt` or `outbound.beforeDeliverPayload` for channel-specific payload lifecycle behavior such as hiding duplicate local approval prompts or sending typing indicators before delivery. 65 - Use `approvalCapability.delivery` only for native approval routing or fallback suppression. 66 - Use `approvalCapability.render` only when a channel truly needs custom approval payloads instead of the shared renderer. 67 - If a channel can infer stable owner-like DM identities from existing config, use `createResolvedApproverActionAuthAdapter` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` to restrict same-chat `/approve` without adding approval-specific core logic. 68 - If a channel needs native approval delivery, keep channel code focused on target normalization and transport hooks. Use `createChannelExecApprovalProfile`, `createChannelNativeOriginTargetResolver`, `createChannelApproverDmTargetResolver`, `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalCapability`, and `createChannelNativeApprovalRuntime` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` so core owns request filtering, routing, dedupe, expiry, and gateway subscription. 69 - Native approval channels must route both `accountId` and `approvalKind` through those helpers. `accountId` keeps multi-account approval policy scoped to the right bot account, and `approvalKind` keeps exec vs plugin approval behavior available to the channel without hardcoded branches in core. 70 - `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalAdapter` still exists as a compatibility wrapper, but new code should prefer the capability builder and expose `approvalCapability` on the plugin. 71 72 Auth-only channels can usually stop at the default path: core handles approvals and the plugin just exposes outbound/auth capabilities. Native approval channels such as Matrix, Slack, Telegram, and custom chat transports should use the shared native helpers instead of rolling their own approval lifecycle. 73 74 ## Walkthrough 75 76 77 78 79 Create the standard plugin files. The `channel` field in `package.json` is 80 what makes this a channel plugin: 81 82 83 ```json package.json 84 { 85 "name": "@myorg/openclaw-acme-chat", 86 "version": "1.0.0", 87 "type": "module", 88 "openclaw": { 89 "extensions": ["./index.ts"], 90 "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts", 91 "channel": { 92 "id": "acme-chat", 93 "label": "Acme Chat", 94 "blurb": "Connect OpenClaw to Acme Chat." 95 } 96 } 97 } 98 ``` 99 100 ```json openclaw.plugin.json 101 { 102 "id": "acme-chat", 103 "kind": "channel", 104 "channels": ["acme-chat"], 105 "name": "Acme Chat", 106 "description": "Acme Chat channel plugin", 107 "configSchema": { 108 "type": "object", 109 "additionalProperties": false, 110 "properties": { 111 "acme-chat": { 112 "type": "object", 113 "properties": { 114 "token": { "type": "string" }, 115 "allowFrom": { 116 "type": "array", 117 "items": { "type": "string" } 118 } 119 } 120 } 121 } 122 } 123 } 124 ``` 125 126 127 128 129 130 The `ChannelPlugin` interface has many optional adapter surfaces. Start with 131 the minimum — `id` and `setup` — and add adapters as you need them. 132 133 Create `src/channel.ts`: 134 135 ```typescript src/channel.ts 136 import { 137 createChatChannelPlugin, 138 createChannelPluginBase, 139 } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 140 import type { OpenClawConfig } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 141 import { acmeChatApi } from "./client.js"; // your platform API client 142 143 type ResolvedAccount = { 144 accountId: string | null; 145 token: string; 146 allowFrom: string[]; 147 dmPolicy: string | undefined; 148 }; 149 150 function resolveAccount( 151 cfg: OpenClawConfig, 152 accountId?: string | null, 153 ): ResolvedAccount { 154 const section = (cfg.channels as Record)?.["acme-chat"]; 155 const token = section?.token; 156 if (!token) throw new Error("acme-chat: token is required"); 157 return { 158 accountId: accountId ?? null, 159 token, 160 allowFrom: section?.allowFrom ?? [], 161 dmPolicy: section?.dmSecurity, 162 }; 163 } 164 165 export const acmeChatPlugin = createChatChannelPlugin({ 166 base: createChannelPluginBase({ 167 id: "acme-chat", 168 setup: { 169 resolveAccount, 170 inspectAccount(cfg, accountId) { 171 const section = 172 (cfg.channels as Record)?.["acme-chat"]; 173 return { 174 enabled: Boolean(section?.token), 175 configured: Boolean(section?.token), 176 tokenStatus: section?.token ? "available" : "missing", 177 }; 178 }, 179 }, 180 }), 181 182 // DM security: who can message the bot 183 security: { 184 dm: { 185 channelKey: "acme-chat", 186 resolvePolicy: (account) => account.dmPolicy, 187 resolveAllowFrom: (account) => account.allowFrom, 188 defaultPolicy: "allowlist", 189 }, 190 }, 191 192 // Pairing: approval flow for new DM contacts 193 pairing: { 194 text: { 195 idLabel: "Acme Chat username", 196 message: "Send this code to verify your identity:", 197 notify: async ({ target, code }) => { 198 await acmeChatApi.sendDm(target, `Pairing code: ${code}`); 199 }, 200 }, 201 }, 202 203 // Threading: how replies are delivered 204 threading: { topLevelReplyToMode: "reply" }, 205 206 // Outbound: send messages to the platform 207 outbound: { 208 attachedResults: { 209 sendText: async (params) => { 210 const result = await acmeChatApi.sendMessage( 211 params.to, 212 params.text, 213 ); 214 return { messageId: result.id }; 215 }, 216 }, 217 base: { 218 sendMedia: async (params) => { 219 await acmeChatApi.sendFile(params.to, params.filePath); 220 }, 221 }, 222 }, 223 }); 224 ``` 225 226 227 Instead of implementing low-level adapter interfaces manually, you pass 228 declarative options and the builder composes them: 229 230 | Option | What it wires | 231 | --- | --- | 232 | `security.dm` | Scoped DM security resolver from config fields | 233 | `pairing.text` | Text-based DM pairing flow with code exchange | 234 | `threading` | Reply-to-mode resolver (fixed, account-scoped, or custom) | 235 | `outbound.attachedResults` | Send functions that return result metadata (message IDs) | 236 237 You can also pass raw adapter objects instead of the declarative options 238 if you need full control. 239 240 241 242 243 244 Create `index.ts`: 245 246 ```typescript index.ts 247 import { defineChannelPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 248 import { acmeChatPlugin } from "./src/channel.js"; 249 250 export default defineChannelPluginEntry({ 251 id: "acme-chat", 252 name: "Acme Chat", 253 description: "Acme Chat channel plugin", 254 plugin: acmeChatPlugin, 255 registerCliMetadata(api) { 256 api.registerCli( 257 ({ program }) => { 258 program 259 .command("acme-chat") 260 .description("Acme Chat management"); 261 }, 262 { 263 descriptors: [ 264 { 265 name: "acme-chat", 266 description: "Acme Chat management", 267 hasSubcommands: false, 268 }, 269 ], 270 }, 271 ); 272 }, 273 registerFull(api) { 274 api.registerGatewayMethod(/* ... */); 275 }, 276 }); 277 ``` 278 279 Put channel-owned CLI descriptors in `registerCliMetadata(...)` so OpenClaw 280 can show them in root help without activating the full channel runtime, 281 while normal full loads still pick up the same descriptors for real command 282 registration. Keep `registerFull(...)` for runtime-only work. 283 `defineChannelPluginEntry` handles the registration-mode split automatically. See 284 [Entry Points](/plugins/sdk-entrypoints#definechannelpluginentry) for all 285 options. 286 287 288 289 290 Create `setup-entry.ts` for lightweight loading during onboarding: 291 292 ```typescript setup-entry.ts 293 import { defineSetupPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 294 import { acmeChatPlugin } from "./src/channel.js"; 295 296 export default defineSetupPluginEntry(acmeChatPlugin); 297 ``` 298 299 OpenClaw loads this instead of the full entry when the channel is disabled 300 or unconfigured. It avoids pulling in heavy runtime code during setup flows. 301 See [Setup and Config](/plugins/sdk-setup#setup-entry) for details. 302 303 304 305 306 Your plugin needs to receive messages from the platform and forward them to 307 OpenClaw. The typical pattern is a webhook that verifies the request and 308 dispatches it through your channel's inbound handler: 309 310 ```typescript 311 registerFull(api) { 312 api.registerHttpRoute({ 313 path: "/acme-chat/webhook", 314 auth: "plugin", // plugin-managed auth (verify signatures yourself) 315 handler: async (req, res) => { 316 const event = parseWebhookPayload(req); 317 318 // Your inbound handler dispatches the message to OpenClaw. 319 // The exact wiring depends on your platform SDK — 320 // see a real example in the bundled Microsoft Teams or Google Chat plugin package. 321 await handleAcmeChatInbound(api, event); 322 323 res.statusCode = 200; 324 res.end("ok"); 325 return true; 326 }, 327 }); 328 } 329 ``` 330 331 332 Inbound message handling is channel-specific. Each channel plugin owns 333 its own inbound pipeline. Look at bundled channel plugins 334 (for example the Microsoft Teams or Google Chat plugin package) for real patterns. 335 336 337 338 339 340 341 Write colocated tests in `src/channel.test.ts`: 342 343 ```typescript src/channel.test.ts 344 import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest"; 345 import { acmeChatPlugin } from "./channel.js"; 346 347 describe("acme-chat plugin", () => { 348 it("resolves account from config", () => { 349 const cfg = { 350 channels: { 351 "acme-chat": { token: "test-token", allowFrom: ["user1"] }, 352 }, 353 } as any; 354 const account = acmeChatPlugin.setup!.resolveAccount(cfg, undefined); 355 expect(account.token).toBe("test-token"); 356 }); 357 358 it("inspects account without materializing secrets", () => { 359 const cfg = { 360 channels: { "acme-chat": { token: "test-token" } }, 361 } as any; 362 const result = acmeChatPlugin.setup!.inspectAccount!(cfg, undefined); 363 expect(result.configured).toBe(true); 364 expect(result.tokenStatus).toBe("available"); 365 }); 366 367 it("reports missing config", () => { 368 const cfg = { channels: {} } as any; 369 const result = acmeChatPlugin.setup!.inspectAccount!(cfg, undefined); 370 expect(result.configured).toBe(false); 371 }); 372 }); 373 ``` 374 375 ```bash 376 pnpm test -- /acme-chat/ 377 ``` 378 379 For shared test helpers, see [Testing](/plugins/sdk-testing). 380 381 382 383 384 ## File structure 385 386 ``` 387 /acme-chat/ 388 ├── package.json # openclaw.channel metadata 389 ├── openclaw.plugin.json # Manifest with config schema 390 ├── index.ts # defineChannelPluginEntry 391 ├── setup-entry.ts # defineSetupPluginEntry 392 ├── api.ts # Public exports (optional) 393 ├── runtime-api.ts # Internal runtime exports (optional) 394 └── src/ 395 ├── channel.ts # ChannelPlugin via createChatChannelPlugin 396 ├── channel.test.ts # Tests 397 ├── client.ts # Platform API client 398 └── runtime.ts # Runtime store (if needed) 399 ``` 400 401 ## Advanced topics 402 403 404 405 Fixed, account-scoped, or custom reply modes 406 407 408 describeMessageTool and action discovery 409 410 411 inferTargetChatType, looksLikeId, resolveTarget 412 413 414 TTS, STT, media, subagent via api.runtime 415 416 417 418 ## Next steps 419 420 - [Provider Plugins](/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins) — if your plugin also provides models 421 - [SDK Overview](/plugins/sdk-overview) — full subpath import reference 422 - [SDK Testing](/plugins/sdk-testing) — test utilities and contract tests 423 - [Plugin Manifest](/plugins/manifest) — full manifest schema 424 Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1	---
2	summary: "Plugin manifest + JSON schema requirements (strict config validation)"
3	read_when:
4	  - You are building an OpenClaw plugin
5	  - You need to ship a plugin config schema or debug plugin validation errors
6	title: "Plugin Manifest"
7	---
8	
9	# Plugin manifest (openclaw.plugin.json)
10	
11	This page is for the **native OpenClaw plugin manifest** only.
12	
13	For compatible bundle layouts, see [Plugin bundles](/plugins/bundles).
14	
15	Compatible bundle formats use different manifest files:
16	
17	- Codex bundle: `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`
18	- Claude bundle: `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` or the default Claude component
19	  layout without a manifest
20	- Cursor bundle: `.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`
21	
22	OpenClaw auto-detects those bundle layouts too, but they are not validated
23	against the `openclaw.plugin.json` schema described here.
24	
25	For compatible bundles, OpenClaw currently reads bundle metadata plus declared
26	skill roots, Claude command roots, Claude bundle `settings.json` defaults, and
27	supported hook packs when the layout matches OpenClaw runtime expectations.
28	
29	Every native OpenClaw plugin **must** ship a `openclaw.plugin.json` file in the
30	**plugin root**. OpenClaw uses this manifest to validate configuration
31	**without executing plugin code**. Missing or invalid manifests are treated as
32	plugin errors and block config validation.
33	
34	See the full plugin system guide: [Plugins](/tools/plugin).
35	For the native capability model and current external-compatibility guidance:
36	[Capability model](/plugins/architecture#public-capability-model).
37	
38	## What this file does
39	
40	`openclaw.plugin.json` is the metadata OpenClaw reads before it loads your
41	plugin code.
42	
43	Use it for:
44	
45	- plugin identity
46	- config validation
47	- auth and onboarding metadata that should be available without booting plugin
48	  runtime
49	- static capability ownership snapshots used for bundled compat wiring and
50	  contract coverage
51	- config UI hints
52	
53	Do not use it for:
54	
55	- registering runtime behavior
56	- declaring code entrypoints
57	- npm install metadata
58	
59	Those belong in your plugin code and `package.json`.
60	
61	## Minimal example
62	
63	```json
64	{
65	  "id": "voice-call",
66	  "configSchema": {
67	    "type": "object",
68	    "additionalProperties": false,
69	    "properties": {}
70	  }
71	}
72	```
73	
74	## Rich example
75	
76	```json
77	{
78	  "id": "openrouter",
79	  "name": "OpenRouter",
80	  "description": "OpenRouter provider plugin",
81	  "version": "1.0.0",
82	  "providers": ["openrouter"],
83	  "cliBackends": ["openrouter-cli"],
84	  "providerAuthEnvVars": {
85	    "openrouter": ["OPENROUTER_API_KEY"]
86	  },
87	  "providerAuthChoices": [
88	    {
89	      "provider": "openrouter",
90	      "method": "api-key",
91	      "choiceId": "openrouter-api-key",
92	      "choiceLabel": "OpenRouter API key",
93	      "groupId": "openrouter",
94	      "groupLabel": "OpenRouter",
95	      "optionKey": "openrouterApiKey",
96	      "cliFlag": "--openrouter-api-key",
97	      "cliOption": "--openrouter-api-key <key>",
98	      "cliDescription": "OpenRouter API key",
99	      "onboardingScopes": ["text-inference"]
100	    }
101	  ],
102	  "uiHints": {
103	    "apiKey": {
104	      "label": "API key",
105	      "placeholder": "sk-or-v1-...",
106	      "sensitive": true
107	    }
108	  },
109	  "configSchema": {
110	    "type": "object",
111	    "additionalProperties": false,
112	    "properties": {
113	      "apiKey": {
114	        "type": "string"
115	      }
116	    }
117	  }
118	}
119	```
120	
121	## Top-level field reference
122	
123	| Field                 | Required | Type                             | What it means                                                                                                                |
124	| --------------------- | -------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
125	| `id`                  | Yes      | `string`                         | Canonical plugin id. This is the id used in `plugins.entries.<id>`.                                                          |
126	| `configSchema`        | Yes      | `object`                         | Inline JSON Schema for this plugin's config.                                                                                 |
127	| `enabledByDefault`    | No       | `true`                           | Marks a bundled plugin as enabled by default. Omit it, or set any non-`true` value, to leave the plugin disabled by default. |
128	| `kind`                | No       | `"memory"` \| `"context-engine"` | Declares an exclusive plugin kind used by `plugins.slots.*`.                                                                 |
129	| `channels`            | No       | `string[]`                       | Channel ids owned by this plugin. Used for discovery and config validation.                                                  |
130	| `providers`           | No       | `string[]`                       | Provider ids owned by this plugin.                                                                                           |
131	| `cliBackends`         | No       | `string[]`                       | CLI inference backend ids owned by this plugin. Used for startup auto-activation from explicit config refs.                  |
132	| `providerAuthEnvVars` | No       | `Record<string, string[]>`       | Cheap provider-auth env metadata that OpenClaw can inspect without loading plugin code.                                      |
133	| `providerAuthChoices` | No       | `object[]`                       | Cheap auth-choice metadata for onboarding pickers, preferred-provider resolution, and simple CLI flag wiring.                |
134	| `contracts`           | No       | `object`                         | Static bundled capability snapshot for speech, media-understanding, image-generation, web search, and tool ownership.        |
135	| `skills`              | No       | `string[]`                       | Skill directories to load, relative to the plugin root.                                                                      |
136	| `name`                | No       | `string`                         | Human-readable plugin name.                                                                                                  |
137	| `description`         | No       | `string`                         | Short summary shown in plugin surfaces.                                                                                      |
138	| `version`             | No       | `string`                         | Informational plugin version.                                                                                                |
139	| `uiHints`             | No       | `Record<string, object>`         | UI labels, placeholders, and sensitivity hints for config fields.                                                            |
140	
141	## providerAuthChoices reference
142	
143	Each `providerAuthChoices` entry describes one onboarding or auth choice.
144	OpenClaw reads this before provider runtime loads.
145	
146	| Field              | Required | Type                                            | What it means                                                                                            |
147	| ------------------ | -------- | ----------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
148	| `provider`         | Yes      | `string`                                        | Provider id this choice belongs to.                                                                      |
149	| `method`           | Yes      | `string`                                        | Auth method id to dispatch to.                                                                           |
150	| `choiceId`         | Yes      | `string`                                        | Stable auth-choice id used by onboarding and CLI flows.                                                  |
151	| `choiceLabel`      | No       | `string`                                        | User-facing label. If omitted, OpenClaw falls back to `choiceId`.                                        |
152	| `choiceHint`       | No       | `string`                                        | Short helper text for the picker.                                                                        |
153	| `groupId`          | No       | `string`                                        | Optional group id for grouping related choices.                                                          |
154	| `groupLabel`       | No       | `string`                                        | User-facing label for that group.                                                                        |
155	| `groupHint`        | No       | `string`                                        | Short helper text for the group.                                                                         |
156	| `optionKey`        | No       | `string`                                        | Internal option key for simple one-flag auth flows.                                                      |
157	| `cliFlag`          | No       | `string`                                        | CLI flag name, such as `--openrouter-api-key`.                                                           |
158	| `cliOption`        | No       | `string`                                        | Full CLI option shape, such as `--openrouter-api-key <key>`.                                             |
159	| `cliDescription`   | No       | `string`                                        | Description used in CLI help.                                                                            |
160	| `onboardingScopes` | No       | `Array<"text-inference" \| "image-generation">` | Which onboarding surfaces this choice should appear in. If omitted, it defaults to `["text-inference"]`. |
161	
162	## uiHints reference
163	
164	`uiHints` is a map from config field names to small rendering hints.
165	
166	```json
167	{
168	  "uiHints": {
169	    "apiKey": {
170	      "label": "API key",
171	      "help": "Used for OpenRouter requests",
172	      "placeholder": "sk-or-v1-...",
173	      "sensitive": true
174	    }
175	  }
176	}
177	```
178	
179	Each field hint can include:
180	
181	| Field         | Type       | What it means                           |
182	| ------------- | ---------- | --------------------------------------- |
183	| `label`       | `string`   | User-facing field label.                |
184	| `help`        | `string`   | Short helper text.                      |
185	| `tags`        | `string[]` | Optional UI tags.                       |
186	| `advanced`    | `boolean`  | Marks the field as advanced.            |
187	| `sensitive`   | `boolean`  | Marks the field as secret or sensitive. |
188	| `placeholder` | `string`   | Placeholder text for form inputs.       |
189	
190	## contracts reference
191	
192	Use `contracts` only for static capability ownership metadata that OpenClaw can
193	read without importing the plugin runtime.
194	
195	```json
196	{
197	  "contracts": {
198	    "speechProviders": ["openai"],
199	    "mediaUnderstandingProviders": ["openai", "openai-codex"],
200	    "imageGenerationProviders": ["openai"],
201	    "webSearchProviders": ["gemini"],
202	    "tools": ["firecrawl_search", "firecrawl_scrape"]
203	  }
204	}
205	```
206	
207	Each list is optional:
208	
209	| Field                         | Type       | What it means                                                  |
210	| ----------------------------- | ---------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
211	| `speechProviders`             | `string[]` | Speech provider ids this plugin owns.                          |
212	| `mediaUnderstandingProviders` | `string[]` | Media-understanding provider ids this plugin owns.             |
213	| `imageGenerationProviders`    | `string[]` | Image-generation provider ids this plugin owns.                |
214	| `webSearchProviders`          | `string[]` | Web-search provider ids this plugin owns.                      |
215	| `tools`                       | `string[]` | Agent tool names this plugin owns for bundled contract checks. |
216	
217	Legacy top-level `speechProviders`, `mediaUnderstandingProviders`, and
218	`imageGenerationProviders` are deprecated. Use `openclaw doctor --fix` to move
219	them under `contracts`; normal manifest loading no longer treats them as
220	capability ownership.
221	
222	## Manifest versus package.json
223	
224	The two files serve different jobs:
225	
226	| File                   | Use it for                                                                                                         |
227	| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
228	| `openclaw.plugin.json` | Discovery, config validation, auth-choice metadata, and UI hints that must exist before plugin code runs           |
229	| `package.json`         | npm metadata, dependency installation, and the `openclaw` block used for entrypoints and setup or catalog metadata |
230	
231	If you are unsure where a piece of metadata belongs, use this rule:
232	
233	- if OpenClaw must know it before loading plugin code, put it in `openclaw.plugin.json`
234	- if it is about packaging, entry files, or npm install behavior, put it in `package.json`
235	
236	## JSON Schema requirements
237	
238	- **Every plugin must ship a JSON Schema**, even if it accepts no config.
239	- An empty schema is acceptable (for example, `{ "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false }`).
240	- Schemas are validated at config read/write time, not at runtime.
241	
242	## Validation behavior
243	
244	- Unknown `channels.*` keys are **errors**, unless the channel id is declared by
245	  a plugin manifest.
246	- `plugins.entries.<id>`, `plugins.allow`, `plugins.deny`, and `plugins.slots.*`
247	  must reference **discoverable** plugin ids. Unknown ids are **errors**.
248	- If a plugin is installed but has a broken or missing manifest or schema,
249	  validation fails and Doctor reports the plugin error.
250	- If plugin config exists but the plugin is **disabled**, the config is kept and
251	  a **warning** is surfaced in Doctor + logs.
252	
253	See [Configuration reference](/gateway/configuration) for the full `plugins.*` schema.
254	
255	## Notes
256	
257	- The manifest is **required for native OpenClaw plugins**, including local filesystem loads.
258	- Runtime still loads the plugin module separately; the manifest is only for
259	  discovery + validation.
260	- Only documented manifest fields are read by the manifest loader. Avoid adding
261	  custom top-level keys here.
262	- `providerAuthEnvVars` is the cheap metadata path for auth probes, env-marker
263	  validation, and similar provider-auth surfaces that should not boot plugin
264	  runtime just to inspect env names.
265	- `providerAuthChoices` is the cheap metadata path for auth-choice pickers,
266	  `--auth-choice` resolution, preferred-provider mapping, and simple onboarding
267	  CLI flag registration before provider runtime loads. For runtime wizard
268	  metadata that requires provider code, see
269	  [Provider runtime hooks](/plugins/architecture#provider-runtime-hooks).
270	- Exclusive plugin kinds are selected through `plugins.slots.*`.
271	  - `kind: "memory"` is selected by `plugins.slots.memory`.
272	  - `kind: "context-engine"` is selected by `plugins.slots.contextEngine`
273	    (default: built-in `legacy`).
274	- `channels`, `providers`, `cliBackends`, and `skills` can be omitted when a
275	  plugin does not need them.
276	- If your plugin depends on native modules, document the build steps and any
277	  package-manager allowlist requirements (for example, pnpm `allow-build-scripts`
278	  - `pnpm rebuild <package>`).
279	
280	## Related
281	
282	- [Building Plugins](/plugins/building-plugins) — getting started with plugins
283	- [Plugin Architecture](/plugins/architecture) — internal architecture
284	- [SDK Overview](/plugins/sdk-overview) — Plugin SDK reference
285

1	# Extensions Boundary
2	
3	This directory contains bundled plugins. Treat it as the same boundary that
4	third-party plugins see.
5	
6	## Public Contracts
7	
8	- Docs:
9	  - `docs/plugins/building-plugins.md`

12	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md`
13	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md`

24	
25	## Boundary Rules
26	
27	- Extension production code should import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` and its
28	  own local barrels such as `./api.ts` and `./runtime-api.ts`.
29	- Do not import core internals from `src/**`, `src/channels/**`,
30	  `src/plugin-sdk-internal/**`, or another extension's `src/**`.
31	- Do not use relative imports that escape the current extension package root.
32	- Keep plugin metadata accurate in `openclaw.plugin.json` and the package
33	  `openclaw` block so discovery and setup work without executing plugin code.
34	- Treat files like `src/**`, `onboard.ts`, and other local helpers as private
35	  unless you intentionally promote them through `api.ts` and, if needed, a
36	  matching `src/plugin-sdk/<id>.ts` facade.
37	- If core or core tests need a bundled plugin helper, export it from `api.ts`
38	  first instead of letting them deep-import extension internals.
39	
40	## Expanding The Boundary
41	
42	- If an extension needs a new seam, add a typed Plugin SDK subpath or additive
43	  export instead of reaching into core.
44	- Keep new plugin-facing seams backwards-compatible and versioned. Third-party
45	  plugins consume this surface.
46	- When intentionally expanding the contract, update the docs, exported subpath
47	  list, package exports, and API/contract checks in the same change.

2	  AllowFromListSchema,
3	  buildChannelConfigSchema,
4	  DmPolicySchema,
5	  MarkdownConfigSchema,
6	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-primitives";
7	import { buildSecretInputSchema } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/secret-input";
8	import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
9	
10	/**
11	 * Validates https:// URLs only (no javascript:, data:, file:, etc.)
12	 */
13	const safeUrlSchema = z
14	  .string()
15	  .url()
16	  .refine(
17	    (url) => {
18	      try {
19	        const parsed = new URL(url);
20	        return parsed.protocol === "https:";
21	      } catch {
22	        return false;
23	      }
24	    },
25	    { message: "URL must use https:// protocol" },
26	  );
27	
28	/**
29	 * NIP-01 profile metadata schema
30	 * https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/01.md
31	 */
32	export const NostrProfileSchema = z.object({
33	  /** Username (NIP-01: name) - max 256 chars */
34	  name: z.string().max(256).optional(),
35	
36	  /** Display name (NIP-01: display_name) - max 256 chars */
37	  displayName: z.string().max(256).optional(),
38	
39	  /** Bio/description (NIP-01: about) - max 2000 chars */
40	  about: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
41	
42	  /** Profile picture URL (must be https) */
43	  picture: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
44	
45	  /** Banner image URL (must be https) */
46	  banner: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
47	
48	  /** Website URL (must be https) */
49	  website: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
50	
51	  /** NIP-05 identifier (e.g., "user@example.com") */
52	  nip05: z.string().optional(),
53	
54	  /** Lightning address (LUD-16) */
55	  lud16: z.string().optional(),
56	});
57	
58	export type NostrProfile = z.infer<typeof NostrProfileSchema>;
59	
60	/**
61	 * Zod schema for channels.nostr.* configuration
62	 */
63	export const NostrConfigSchema = z.object({
64	  /** Account name (optional display name) */
65	  name: z.string().optional(),
66	
67	  /** Optional default account id for routing/account selection. */
68	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
69	
70	  /** Whether this channel is enabled */
71	  enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
72	
73	  /** Markdown formatting overrides (tables). */
74	  markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
75	
76	  /** Private key in hex or nsec bech32 format */
77	  privateKey: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
78	
79	  /** WebSocket relay URLs to connect to */
80	  relays: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
81	
82	  /** DM access policy: pairing, allowlist, open, or disabled */
83	  dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional(),
84	
85	  /** Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex format) */
86	  allowFrom: AllowFromListSchema,
87	
88	  /** Profile metadata (NIP-01 kind:0 content) */
89	  profile: NostrProfileSchema.optional(),
90	});
91	
92	export type NostrConfig = z.infer<typeof NostrConfigSchema>;
93	
94	/**
95	 * JSON Schema for Control UI (converted from Zod)
96	 */
97	export const nostrChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(NostrConfigSchema);

1	import {
2	  AllowFromListSchema,
3	  buildChannelConfigSchema,
4	  DmPolicySchema,
5	  MarkdownConfigSchema,
6	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-primitives";
7	import { buildSecretInputSchema } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/secret-input";
8	import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
9	
10	/**
11	 * Validates https:// URLs only (no javascript:, data:, file:, etc.)
12	 */
13	const safeUrlSchema = z
14	  .string()
15	  .url()
16	  .refine(
17	    (url) => {
18	      try {
19	        const parsed = new URL(url);
20	        return parsed.protocol === "https:";
21	      } catch {
22	        return false;
23	      }
24	    },
25	    { message: "URL must use https:// protocol" },
26	  );
27	
28	/**
29	 * NIP-01 profile metadata schema
30	 * https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/01.md
31	 */
32	export const NostrProfileSchema = z.object({
33	  /** Username (NIP-01: name) - max 256 chars */
34	  name: z.string().max(256).optional(),
35	
36	  /** Display name (NIP-01: display_name) - max 256 chars */
37	  displayName: z.string().max(256).optional(),
38	
39	  /** Bio/description (NIP-01: about) - max 2000 chars */
40	  about: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
41	
42	  /** Profile picture URL (must be https) */
43	  picture: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
44	
45	  /** Banner image URL (must be https) */
46	  banner: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
47	
48	  /** Website URL (must be https) */
49	  website: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
50	
51	  /** NIP-05 identifier (e.g., "user@example.com") */
52	  nip05: z.string().optional(),
53	
54	  /** Lightning address (LUD-16) */
55	  lud16: z.string().optional(),
56	});
57	
58	export type NostrProfile = z.infer<typeof NostrProfileSchema>;
59	
60	/**
61	 * Zod schema for channels.nostr.* configuration
62	 */
63	export const NostrConfigSchema = z.object({
64	  /** Account name (optional display name) */
65	  name: z.string().optional(),
66	
67	  /** Optional default account id for routing/account selection. */
68	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
69	
70	  /** Whether this channel is enabled */
71	  enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
72	
73	  /** Markdown formatting overrides (tables). */
74	  markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
75	
76	  /** Private key in hex or nsec bech32 format */
77	  privateKey: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
78	
79	  /** WebSocket relay URLs to connect to */
80	  relays: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
81	
82	  /** DM access policy: pairing, allowlist, open, or disabled */
83	  dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional(),
84	
85	  /** Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex format) */
86	  allowFrom: AllowFromListSchema,
87	
88	  /** Profile metadata (NIP-01 kind:0 content) */
89	  profile: NostrProfileSchema.optional(),
90	});
91	
92	export type NostrConfig = z.infer<typeof NostrConfigSchema>;
93	
94	/**
95	 * JSON Schema for Control UI (converted from Zod)
96	 */
97	export const nostrChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(NostrConfigSchema);
98

1	import type { ResolvedIrcAccount } from "./accounts.js";
2	import type { IrcClientOptions } from "./client.js";

4	type IrcConnectOverrides = Omit<
5	  Partial<IrcClientOptions>,
6	  "host" | "port" | "tls" | "nick" | "username" | "realname" | "password" | "nickserv"
7	>;
8	
9	export function buildIrcConnectOptions(
10	  account: ResolvedIrcAccount,
11	  overrides: IrcConnectOverrides = {},
12	): IrcClientOptions {
13	  return {
14	    host: account.host,
15	    port: account.port,
16	    tls: account.tls,
17	    nick: account.nick,
18	    username: account.username,
19	    realname: account.realname,
20	    password: account.password,
21	    nickserv: {
22	      enabled: account.config.nickserv?.enabled,
23	      service: account.config.nickserv?.service,
24	      password: account.config.nickserv?.password,
25	      register: account.config.nickserv?.register,
26	      registerEmail: account.config.nickserv?.registerEmail,
27	    },
28	    ...overrides,
29	  };
30	}

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive summary document:

## Complete Channel Plugin Implementation Reference

I've found and analyzed complete channel plugin implementations in the OpenClaw codebase. Here's a comprehensive guide for creating your "PulseBoard" channel plugin template.

---

## PLUGIN COMPLEXITY OVERVIEW

**IRC Plugin** (simpler, protocol-based)
- 34 TypeScript source files
- ~200 KB total code
- Focus: Direct network protocol integration
- Good for: Custom messaging protocols, network APIs

**Nostr Plugin** (medium complexity, decentralized)
- 27 TypeScript source files  
- ~180 KB total code
- Focus: Cryptographic DMs, relay networks
- Good for: Privacy-focused channels, distributed systems

**Other Available Plugins** (for reference)
- Discord: 254 files (most complex - real-time API)
- Telegram: 200 files
- Slack: 178 files  
- WhatsApp: 120 files (simpler - webhook-based)

---

## IRC PLUGIN - COMPLETE FILE STRUCTURE

**Location:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775467524242-gn0vo7/extensions/irc/`

### Root Configuration Files

**package.json** - Plugin metadata and entry points
- Name: `@openclaw/irc`
- Version: `2026.4.1-beta.1`
- Entry points:
  - `./index.ts` - Main plugin entry
  - `./setup-entry.ts` - Setup wizard entry

**openclaw.plugin.json** - Plugin registration manifest
```json
{
  "id": "irc",
  "channels": ["irc"],
  "configSchema": { "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false, "properties": {} }
}
```

**index.ts** - Main entry point
- Exports: `ircPlugin`, `setIrcRuntime`
- Calls: `defineChannelPluginEntry()` with plugin and runtime setter

**setup-entry.ts** - Setup wizard integration
- Calls: `defineSetupPluginEntry(ircPlugin)`

**api.ts** - Public API barrel
- Exports: Account helpers, setup surface

**channel-config-api.ts** - Config schema exports
- Exports: `IrcChannelConfigSchema`

**runtime-api.ts** - Private runtime barrel
- Re-exports from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc`

---

### Core Source Files (`src/`)

**channel.ts** (358 lines) - Main plugin definition
- Creates `ircPlugin` with `createChatChannelPlugin()`
- Defines capabilities: `chatTypes: ["direct", "group"]`, media support
- Handles groups, messaging targets, directory, status
- Sets up pairing, DM security policies
- Configures inbound/outbound routing
- Key patterns:
  ```typescript
  createChatChannelPlugin({
    base: { id, meta, setup, setupWizard, capabilities, ... },
    pairing: { text: { ... } },
    security: { resolveDmPolicy, collectWarnings },
    outbound: { base: { deliveryMode, chunker }, attachedResults: { ... } }
  })
  ```

**types.ts** (101 lines) - TypeScript type definitions
- `IrcChannelConfig` - Per-group channel config
- `IrcNickServConfig` - NickServ authentication
- `IrcAccountConfig` - Single account config
- `IrcConfig` - Multi-account config wrapper
- `CoreConfig` - Full OpenClaw config with IRC section
- `IrcInboundMessage` - Message structure from IRC network
- `IrcProbe` - Connection health check result

**config-schema.ts** (99 lines) - Zod validation schemas
- `IrcGroupSchema` - Channel-specific policies (mention, tools, allowFrom)
- `IrcNickServSchema` - NickServ config validation
- `IrcAccountSchemaBase` - Base account config schema
- `IrcAccountSchema` - Account schema with refinements
- `IrcConfigSchema` - Root config schema
- `IrcChannelConfigSchema` - Final channel config with UI hints

**accounts.ts** (226 lines) - Account resolution
- `ResolvedIrcAccount` type - All needed account state
- `listIrcAccountIds()` - List all configured accounts
- `resolveDefaultIrcAccountId()` - Get default account
- `resolveIrcAccount()` - Merge config + resolve passwords/env vars
- `listEnabledIrcAccounts()` - Filtered enabled accounts
- Handles password resolution from: env vars, password files, config

**client.ts** (440 lines) - Low-level IRC protocol client
- `connectIrcClient()` - Async connect with timeout
- `IrcClient` interface - Methods: `sendRaw()`, `join()`, `sendPrivmsg()`, `quit()`, `close()`
- Socket-based implementation (TLS or plain TCP)
- IRC protocol parsing: PING, NICK collision recovery, PRIVMSG handling
- NickServ IDENTIFY/REGISTER support
- State machine: connection → auth → ready
- Handles connection timeouts and graceful shutdown

**inbound.ts** (360 lines) - Inbound message routing
- `handleIrcInbound()` - Main async message handler
- Checks: DM policy, group access, allowlists, mention requirements
- Creates pairing challenges for unauthorized senders
- Builds inbound context payload
- Dispatches to agent runtime via `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase()`
- Handles both DM and group messages
- Integrates with policy checkers

**normalize.ts** (124 lines) - Identifier normalization
- `isChannelTarget()` - Check if target starts with # or &
- `normalizeIrcMessagingTarget()` - Parse "irc:", "channel:", "user:" prefixes
- `looksLikeIrcTargetId()` - Validate target format
- `normalizeIrcAllowEntry()` - Normalize allow-list entries
- `normalizeIrcAllowlist()` - Process arrays of entries
- `buildIrcAllowlistCandidates()` - Build matching variants (nick, nick!user, nick@host, full)
- `resolveIrcAllowlistMatch()` - Check if sender matches allowlist

**send.ts** (102 lines) - Outbound message sending
- `sendMessageIrc()` - Send DM/message to IRC target
- Resolves account, validates target, applies markdown formatting
- Reuses persistent connection or creates transient one
- Records activity in runtime
- Returns `SendIrcResult` with messageId and target

**monitor.ts** (146 lines) - Connection monitor loop
- `monitorIrcProvider()` - Async loop connecting to IRC server
- Handles all incoming PRIVMSG events
- Invokes `handleIrcInbound()` for each message
- Records inbound/outbound activity
- Returns stop() function for graceful shutdown
- Manages connection lifecycle

**policy.ts** (167 lines) - Access control logic
- `resolveIrcGroupMatch()` - Find group config by channel name (case-insensitive)
- `resolveIrcGroupAccessGate()` - Check if group is accessible (policy checks)
- `resolveIrcRequireMention()` - Determine if group requires @mention
- `resolveIrcMentionGate()` - Check if message should be skipped for mention
- `resolveIrcGroupSenderAllowed()` - Check sender against allowlists
- Implements: allowlist policy, open policy, disabled policy

**probe.ts** (54 lines) - Connection health check
- `probeIrc()` - Test connection to IRC server
- Returns `IrcProbe` with status (ok), latency, error message
- 8-second timeout by default
- Tests actual connection, doesn't mock

**protocol.ts** (170 lines) - IRC protocol handling
- `parseIrcLine()` - Parse raw IRC line format (`:prefix CMD params :trailing`)
- `parseIrcPrefix()` - Extract nick, user, host from prefix
- `sanitizeIrcOutboundText()` - Remove control chars, decode escapes
- `sanitizeIrcTarget()` - Validate target (nick or channel)
- `splitIrcText()` - Split text to max chars (word-wrapped)
- `makeIrcMessageId()` - Generate UUID for messages

**control-chars.ts** (23 lines) - Control character filtering
- `isIrcControlChar()` - Check for control bytes (0x00-0x1F, 0x7F)
- `hasIrcControlChars()` - Test if string contains any
- `stripIrcControlChars()` - Remove all control characters

**setup-surface.ts** (partial) - Setup wizard UI
- Interactive prompts for: host, nick, TLS, channels
- NickServ configuration wizard
- Allowlist configuration
- Group/channel access setup

**setup-core.ts** (140 lines) - Setup configuration builders
- Helper functions to patch IRC config
- `ircSetupAdapter` - Channel setup adapter interface
- `setIrcDmPolicy()`, `setIrcAllowFrom()`, `setIrcNickServ()`
- `updateIrcAccountConfig()` - Generic config patcher
- Validates host and nick are present

**config-ui-hints.ts** (41 lines) - UI metadata
- Human-readable labels for config fields
- Help text for UI context
- Hints for: dmPolicy, NickServ options

**connect-options.ts** (31 lines) - Connection builder
- `buildIrcConnectOptions()` - Convert ResolvedIrcAccount to IrcClientOptions
- Prepares all needed connection parameters

**config-schema.ts** - Full Zod schemas for validation

**runtime.ts** (10 lines) - Runtime store initialization
- Simple store getter/setter for IRC runtime
- `getIrcRuntime()`, `setIrcRuntime()`

---

### Test Files (in `src/`)

**channel.test.ts** - Plugin definition tests
**client.test.ts** - Client connection tests  
**accounts.test.ts** - Account resolution tests
**config-schema.test.ts** - Config validation tests
**inbound.behavior.test.ts** - Inbound behavior tests
**inbound.policy.test.ts** - Policy enforcement tests
**policy.test.ts** - Access control logic tests
**probe.test.ts** - Health check tests
**protocol.test.ts** - IRC protocol parsing tests
**normalize.test.ts** - Target normalization tests
**setup.test.ts** - Setup wizard tests
**send.test.ts** - Message sending tests
**monitor.test.ts** - Monitor loop tests
**control-chars.test.ts** - Control char filtering tests
**connect-options.test.ts** - Connection builder tests

---

## NOSTR PLUGIN - COMPLETE FILE STRUCTURE

**Location:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775467524242-gn0vo7/extensions/nostr/`

### Root Configuration Files

**package.json** - Plugin metadata
- Name: `@openclaw/nostr`
- Dependencies: `nostr-tools@^2.23.3`
- Entry points: `./index.ts`, `./setup-entry.ts`
- NPM publish enabled

**openclaw.plugin.json** - Registration manifest
```json
{
  "id": "nostr",
  "channels": ["nostr"],
  "configSchema": { "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false, "properties": {} }
}
```

**index.ts** (66 lines) - Main entry point
- Exports: `nostrPlugin`, `setNostrRuntime`
- `registerFull()` hook for HTTP route registration
- Creates HTTP handler for profile sync (`/api/channels/nostr`)

**setup-entry.ts** - Setup integration
**api.ts** - Public API barrel (re-exports runtime-api)
**runtime-api.ts** (174 lines) - Private runtime barrel
**test-api.ts** - Test exports

---

### Core Source Files (`src/`)

**channel.ts** (partial 200 lines shown) - Main plugin definition
- Creates `nostrPlugin` with `createChatChannelPlugin()`
- Capabilities: `chatTypes: ["direct"]` (DMs only)
- No media support (MVP)
- Handles Nostr pubkey normalization
- DM access resolution with pre-crypto guards
- Status adapter for relay connectivity
- Session route resolution

**types.ts** (117 lines) - Type definitions
- `NostrAccountConfig` - Account configuration
- `ResolvedNostrAccount` - Resolved account with derived data
- Account list/default resolution helpers
- `listNostrAccountIds()` - List configured accounts
- `resolveDefaultNostrAccountId()` - Get default account
- `resolveNostrAccount()` - Merge config, derive publicKey from privateKey

**config-schema.ts** (98 lines) - Zod validation schemas
- `NostrProfileSchema` - NIP-01 metadata (name, displayName, about, picture, banner, website, nip05, lud16)
- `NostrConfigSchema` - Channel config with privateKey, relays, dmPolicy, allowFrom, profile
- Safe URL validation (https:// only)
- Field length constraints (NIP-01 compliant)

**setup-surface.ts** (100+ lines shown) - Setup wizard
- Interactive prompts for private key (hex or nsec format)
- Relay URL configuration with validation (wss:// or ws://)
- Allow-list configuration with pubkey validation
- Help text with examples

**runtime.ts** (7 lines) - Runtime store
- `getNostrRuntime()`, `setNostrRuntime()`

**nostr-bus.ts** (100+ lines shown) - Nostr relay communication
- Uses `nostr-tools` library (SimplePool, finalizeEvent, verifyEvent, nip19)
- `NostrBusOptions` interface with callbacks
- Constants: STARTUP_LOOKBACK_SEC (120), circuit breaker thresholds
- Health tracking with fixed-window rate limiters
- Seen event tracker for deduplication
- State persistence (bus state, profile state, seen events)
- Metrics collection

**session-route.ts** (26 lines) - Outbound session routing
- `resolveNostrOutboundSessionRoute()` - Build session route
- Strips "nostr:" prefix from target
- Routes to direct DM peer

**default-relays.ts** (1 line)
- `DEFAULT_RELAYS = ["wss://relay.damus.io", "wss://nos.lol"]`

**metrics.ts** (12 KB) - Metrics collection
- Event tracking: connection, disconnection, relay state, EOSE
- Metrics snapshots for status reporting

**nostr-profile.ts** - Profile metadata handling
**nostr-profile-http.ts** - HTTP handler for profile sync
**nostr-profile-import.ts** - Profile import utilities
**nostr-state-store.ts** - State persistence
**seen-tracker.ts** - Deduplication tracking

### Test Files

- `channel.test.ts` - Plugin definition tests
- `channel.inbound.test.ts` - Inbound message tests
- `channel.outbound.test.ts` - Outbound send tests
- `nostr-bus.test.ts` - Bus initialization tests
- `nostr-bus.inbound.test.ts` - Bus inbound tests
- `nostr-bus.integration.test.ts` - Integration tests
- `nostr-bus.fuzz.test.ts` - Fuzz tests
- `nostr-profile.test.ts` - Profile handling tests
- `nostr-profile-http.test.ts` - HTTP handler tests
- `nostr-state-store.test.ts` - State persistence tests

---

## PLUGIN CREATION CHECKLIST FOR PULSEBOARD

Based on the IRC and Nostr patterns, your PulseBoard plugin needs:

### 1. **Root Directory Structure**
```
extensions/pulseboard/
├── package.json                    # npm metadata + openclaw config
├── openclaw.plugin.json            # Plugin registration
├── index.ts                        # Main entry point (defineChannelPluginEntry)
├── setup-entry.ts                 # Setup wizard entry (defineSetupPluginEntry)
├── api.ts                         # Public API barrel
├── channel-config-api.ts          # Config schema exports (optional)
├── runtime-api.ts                 # Private runtime barrel
├── setup-api.ts                   # Setup exports (optional)
└── src/
    ├── channel.ts                 # Main plugin definition (createChatChannelPlugin)
    ├── types.ts                   # Type definitions
    ├── config-schema.ts           # Zod validation schemas
    ├── accounts.ts                # Account resolution
    ├── client.ts                  # Protocol client (or API wrapper)
    ├── inbound.ts                 # Inbound message handling
    ├── send.ts                    # Outbound message sending
    ├── normalize.ts               # Identifier/target normalization
    ├── setup-surface.ts           # Setup wizard UI prompts
    ├── setup-core.ts              # Setup config builders
    ├── monitor.ts                 # Connection/polling loop
    ├── policy.ts                  # Access control logic
    ├── probe.ts                   # Health check
    ├── runtime.ts                 # Runtime store
    ├── protocol.ts                # Protocol parsing (if applicable)
    ├── config-ui-hints.ts         # UI metadata
    ├── [service].ts               # Service-specific modules
    ├── *.test.ts                  # Test files
```

### 2. **Mandatory Files by Functionality**

**Always Required:**
- `package.json` with `"openclaw"` section
- `openclaw.plugin.json` with id and channels array
- `index.ts` with `defineChannelPluginEntry()`
- `setup-entry.ts` with `defineSetupPluginEntry()`
- `api.ts` (even if empty barrel)
- `runtime-api.ts` (even if just re-exports)
- `src/types.ts` - Type definitions
- `src/channel.ts` - Main plugin creation
- `src/config-schema.ts` - Zod schemas
- `src/accounts.ts` - Account resolution logic
- `src/runtime.ts` - Runtime store

**For Direct Messages:**
- `src/inbound.ts` - DM handling
- `src/send.ts` - DM sending

**For Groups/Channels:**
- `src/policy.ts` - Group access control
- Extend `inbound.ts` for group messages

**For Setup Wizard:**
- `src/setup-surface.ts` - Interactive prompts
- `src/setup-core.ts` - Config application

**For External APIs/Protocols:**
- `src/client.ts` or `src/protocol.ts` - API wrapper
- `src/monitor.ts` - Connection/polling loop
- `src/normalize.ts` - Input normalization
- `src/probe.ts` - Health check

**Optional:**
- `src/config-ui-hints.ts` - Human-readable labels
- Channel-specific modules (metrics, profiles, etc.)

### 3. **Key Interface Implementations**

Every plugin must return these from `createChatChannelPlugin()`:

```typescript
{
  base: {
    id: "pulseboard",
    meta: { label, blurb, docsPath, ... },
    capabilities: { chatTypes: ["direct"|"group"], media: boolean, ... },
    configSchema: ZodSchema,
    config: { isConfigured, describeAccount, ... },
    messaging: { normalizeTarget, targetResolver, ... },
    setup: ChannelSetupAdapter,
    setupWizard: ChannelSetupWizard,
    // Optional:
    groups: { resolveRequireMention, resolveToolPolicy, ... },
    directory: { listPeers, listGroups },
    status: ComputedAccountStatusAdapter,
    resolver: { resolveTargets },
    gateway: { startAccount },
  },
  pairing?: { text: { idLabel, message, normalizeAllowEntry, notify } },
  security?: { resolveDmPolicy, collectWarnings },
  outbound: {
    base: { deliveryMode, chunker, chunkerMode, textChunkLimit },
    attachedResults: { channel, sendText, sendMedia },
  },
}
```

### 4. **File Size Reference**

**Expected file sizes for similar complexity:**
- `channel.ts` - 200-400 lines
- `client.ts` or `protocol.ts` - 300-500 lines
- `inbound.ts` - 200-400 lines
- `config-schema.ts` - 80-150 lines
- `types.ts` - 100-150 lines
- `accounts.ts` - 150-250 lines
- `setup-surface.ts` - 200-400 lines
- `send.ts` - 50-150 lines
- `normalize.ts` - 100-200 lines
- Other small helpers - 20-80 lines each

---

## SDK IMPORTS BY FUNCTIONALITY

**Core Plugin Creation:**
```typescript
import { createChatChannelPlugin, defineChannelPluginEntry, defineSetupPluginEntry } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core";
```

**Configuration:**
```typescript
import { buildChannelConfigSchema, ... } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-schema";
import { createScopedChannelConfigAdapter, createScopedDmSecurityResolver, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-helpers";
```

**Account Management:**
```typescript
import { createAccountListHelpers, normalizeAccountId, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/account-helpers";
import { resolveMergedAccountConfig } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/account-resolution";
```

**Message Handling:**
```typescript
import { dispatchInboundReplyWithBase, deliverFormattedTextWithAttachments, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc"; // or equivalent
```

**Setup Wizard:**
```typescript
import { createSetupPluginEntry, createPromptParsedAllowFromForAccount, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/setup";
```

**Status/Health:**
```typescript
import { createComputedAccountStatusAdapter, createDefaultChannelRuntimeState, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/status-helpers";
```

**Utilities:**
```typescript
import { createPluginRuntimeStore } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/runtime-store";
import { runStoppablePassiveMonitor } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/extension-shared";
import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
```

---

## CONFIGURATION PATTERN (package.json openclaw block)

```json
{
  "openclaw": {
    "extensions": ["./index.ts"],
    "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts",
    "channel": {
      "id": "pulseboard",
      "label": "PulseBoard",
      "selectionLabel": "PulseBoard (API Key)",
      "docsPath": "/channels/pulseboard",
      "docsLabel": "pulseboard",
      "blurb": "Real-time messaging via PulseBoard API.",
      "systemImage": "network",
      "quickstartAllowFrom": true
    },
    "install": {
      "minHostVersion": ">=2026.4.1"
    }
  }
}
```

---

## PLUGIN MANIFEST PATTERN (openclaw.plugin.json)

```json
{
  "id": "pulseboard",
  "channels": ["pulseboard"],
  "configSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "additionalProperties": false,
    "properties": {}
  }
}
```

---

This reference covers the complete structure, patterns, and requirements for implementing a channel plugin following the IRC and Nostr templates. Use this to create your PulseBoard plugin with all necessary files and integrations.

- `IrcNickServConfig` - NickServ authentication
- `IrcAccountConfig` - Single account config
- `IrcConfig` - Multi-account config wrapper
- `CoreConfig` - Full OpenClaw config with IRC section
- `IrcInboundMessage` - Message structure from IRC network
- `IrcProbe` - Connection health check result

**config-schema.ts** (99 lines) - Zod validation schemas
- `IrcGroupSchema` - Channel-specific policies (mention, tools, allowFrom)
- `IrcNickServSchema` - NickServ config validation
- `IrcAccountSchemaBase` - Base account config schema
- `IrcAccountSchema` - Account schema with refinements
- `IrcConfigSchema` - Root config schema
- `IrcChannelConfigSchema` - Final channel config with UI hints

**accounts.ts** (226 lines) - Account resolution
- `ResolvedIrcAccount` type - All needed account state
- `listIrcAccountIds()` - List all configured accounts
- `resolveDefaultIrcAccountId()` - Get default account
- `resolveIrcAccount()` - Merge config + resolve passwords/env vars
- `listEnabledIrcAccounts()` - Filtered enabled accounts
- Handles password resolution from: env vars, password files, config

**client.ts** (440 lines) - Low-level IRC protocol client
- `connectIrcClient()` - Async connect with timeout
- `IrcClient` interface - Methods: `sendRaw()`, `join()`, `sendPrivmsg()`, `quit()`, `close()`
- Socket-based implementation (TLS or plain TCP)
- IRC protocol parsing: PING, NICK collision recovery, PRIVMSG handling
- NickServ IDENTIFY/REGISTER support
- State machine: connection → auth → ready
- Handles connection timeouts and graceful shutdown

**inbound.ts** (360 lines) - Inbound message routing
- `handleIrcInbound()` - Main async message handler
- Checks: DM policy, group access, allowlists, mention requirements
- Creates pairing challenges for unauthorized senders
- Builds inbound context payload
- Dispatches to agent runtime via `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase()`
- Handles both DM and group messages
- Integrates with policy checkers

**normalize.ts** (124 lines) - Identifier normalization
- `isChannelTarget()` - Check if target starts with # or &
- `normalizeIrcMessagingTarget()` - Parse "irc:", "channel:", "user:" prefixes
- `looksLikeIrcTargetId()` - Validate target format
- `normalizeIrcAllowEntry()` - Normalize allow-list entries
- `normalizeIrcAllowlist()` - Process arrays of entries
- `buildIrcAllowlistCandidates()` - Build matching variants (nick, nick!user, nick@host, full)
- `resolveIrcAllowlistMatch()` - Check if sender matches allowlist

**send.ts** (102 lines) - Outbound message sending
- `sendMessageIrc()` - Send DM/message to IRC target
- Resolves account, validates target, applies markdown formatting
- Reuses persistent connection or creates transient one
- Records activity in runtime
- Returns `SendIrcResult` with messageId and target

**monitor.ts** (146 lines) - Connection monitor loop
- `monitorIrcProvider()` - Async loop connecting to IRC server
- Handles all incoming PRIVMSG events
- Invokes `handleIrcInbound()` for each message
- Records inbound/outbound activity
- Returns stop() function for graceful shutdown
- Manages connection lifecycle

**policy.ts** (167 lines) - Access control logic
- `resolveIrcGroupMatch()` - Find group config by channel name (case-insensitive)
- `resolveIrcGroupAccessGate()` - Check if group is accessible (policy checks)
- `resolveIrcRequireMention()` - Determine if group requires @mention
- `resolveIrcMentionGate()` - Check if message should be skipped for mention
- `resolveIrcGroupSenderAllowed()` - Check sender against allowlists
- Implements: allowlist policy, open policy, disabled policy

**probe.ts** (54 lines) - Connection health check
- `probeIrc()` - Test connection to IRC server
- Returns `IrcProbe` with status (ok), latency, error message
- 8-second timeout by default
- Tests actual connection, doesn't mock

**protocol.ts** (170 lines) - IRC protocol handling
- `parseIrcLine()` - Parse raw IRC line format (`:prefix CMD params :trailing`)
- `parseIrcPrefix()` - Extract nick, user, host from prefix
- `sanitizeIrcOutboundText()` - Remove control chars, decode escapes
- `sanitizeIrcTarget()` - Validate target (nick or channel)
- `splitIrcText()` - Split text to max chars (word-wrapped)
- `makeIrcMessageId()` - Generate UUID for messages

**control-chars.ts** (23 lines) - Control character filtering
- `isIrcControlChar()` - Check for control bytes (0x00-0x1F, 0x7F)
- `hasIrcControlChars()` - Test if string contains any
- `stripIrcControlChars()` - Remove all control characters

**setup-surface.ts** (partial) - Setup wizard UI
- Interactive prompts for: host, nick, TLS, channels
- NickServ configuration wizard
- Allowlist configuration
- Group/channel access setup

**setup-core.ts** (140 lines) - Setup configuration builders
- Helper functions to patch IRC config
- `ircSetupAdapter` - Channel setup adapter interface
- `setIrcDmPolicy()`, `setIrcAllowFrom()`, `setIrcNickServ()`
- `updateIrcAccountConfig()` - Generic config patcher
- Validates host and nick are present

**config-ui-hints.ts** (41 lines) - UI metadata
- Human-readable labels for config fields
- Help text for UI context
- Hints for: dmPolicy, NickServ options

**connect-options.ts** (31 lines) - Connection builder
- `buildIrcConnectOptions()` - Convert ResolvedIrcAccount to IrcClientOptions
- Prepares all needed connection parameters

**config-schema.ts** - Full Zod schemas for validation

**runtime.ts** (10 lines) - Runtime store initialization
- Simple store getter/setter for IRC runtime
- `getIrcRuntime()`, `setIrcRuntime()`

---

### Test Files (in `src/`)

**channel.test.ts** - Plugin definition tests
**client.test.ts** - Client connection tests  
**accounts.test.ts** - Account resolution tests
**config-schema.test.ts** - Config validation tests
**inbound.behavior.test.ts** - Inbound behavior tests
**inbound.policy.test.ts** - Policy enforcement tests
**policy.test.ts** - Access control logic tests
**probe.test.ts** - Health check tests
**protocol.test.ts** - IRC protocol parsing tests
**normalize.test.ts** - Target normalization tests
**setup.test.ts** - Setup wizard tests
**send.test.ts** - Message sending tests
**monitor.test.ts** - Monitor loop tests
**control-chars.test.ts** - Control char filtering tests
**connect-options.test.ts** - Connection builder tests

**Location:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775467524242-gn0vo7/extensions/nostr/`


**setup-entry.ts** - Setup integration

- `NostrConfigSchema` - Channel config with privateKey, relays, dmPolicy, allowFrom, profile

## PLUGIN CREATION CHECKLIST FOR PULSEBOARD

Based on the IRC and Nostr patterns, your PulseBoard plugin needs:

### 1. **Root Directory Structure**
```
extensions/pulseboard/
├── package.json                    # npm metadata + openclaw config
├── openclaw.plugin.json            # Plugin registration
├── index.ts                        # Main entry point (defineChannelPluginEntry)
├── setup-entry.ts                 # Setup wizard entry (defineSetupPluginEntry)
├── api.ts                         # Public API barrel
├── channel-config-api.ts          # Config schema exports (optional)
├── runtime-api.ts                 # Private runtime barrel
├── setup-api.ts                   # Setup exports (optional)
└── src/
    ├── channel.ts                 # Main plugin definition (createChatChannelPlugin)
    ├── types.ts                   # Type definitions
    ├── config-schema.ts           # Zod validation schemas
    ├── accounts.ts                # Account resolution
    ├── client.ts                  # Protocol client (or API wrapper)
    ├── inbound.ts                 # Inbound message handling
    ├── send.ts                    # Outbound message sending
    ├── normalize.ts               # Identifier/target normalization
    ├── setup-surface.ts           # Setup wizard UI prompts
    ├── setup-core.ts              # Setup config builders
    ├── monitor.ts                 # Connection/polling loop
    ├── policy.ts                  # Access control logic
    ├── probe.ts                   # Health check
    ├── runtime.ts                 # Runtime store
    ├── protocol.ts                # Protocol parsing (if applicable)
    ├── config-ui-hints.ts         # UI metadata
    ├── [service].ts               # Service-specific modules
    ├── *.test.ts                  # Test files
```

### 2. **Mandatory Files by Functionality**

**Always Required:**
- `package.json` with `"openclaw"` section
- `openclaw.plugin.json` with id and channels array
- `index.ts` with `defineChannelPluginEntry()`
- `setup-entry.ts` with `defineSetupPluginEntry()`
- `api.ts` (even if empty barrel)
- `runtime-api.ts` (even if just re-exports)
- `src/types.ts` - Type definitions
- `src/channel.ts` - Main plugin creation
- `src/config-schema.ts` - Zod schemas
- `src/accounts.ts` - Account resolution logic
- `src/runtime.ts` - Runtime store

**For Direct Messages:**
- `src/inbound.ts` - DM handling
- `src/send.ts` - DM sending

**For Groups/Channels:**
- `src/policy.ts` - Group access control
- Extend `inbound.ts` for group messages

**For Setup Wizard:**
- `src/setup-surface.ts` - Interactive prompts
- `src/setup-core.ts` - Config application

**For External APIs/Protocols:**
- `src/client.ts` or `src/protocol.ts` - API wrapper
- `src/monitor.ts` - Connection/polling loop
- `src/normalize.ts` - Input normalization
- `src/probe.ts` - Health check

**Optional:**
- `src/config-ui-hints.ts` - Human-readable labels
- Channel-specific modules (metrics, profiles, etc.)

### 3. **Key Interface Implementations**

Every plugin must return these from `createChatChannelPlugin()`:

```typescript
{
  base: {
    id: "pulseboard",
    meta: { label, blurb, docsPath, ... },
    capabilities: { chatTypes: ["direct"|"group"], media: boolean, ... },
    configSchema: ZodSchema,
    config: { isConfigured, describeAccount, ... },
    messaging: { normalizeTarget, targetResolver, ... },
    setup: ChannelSetupAdapter,
    setupWizard: ChannelSetupWizard,
    // Optional:
    groups: { resolveRequireMention, resolveToolPolicy, ... },
    directory: { listPeers, listGroups },
    status: ComputedAccountStatusAdapter,
    resolver: { resolveTargets },
    gateway: { startAccount },
  },
  pairing?: { text: { idLabel, message, normalizeAllowEntry, notify } },
  security?: { resolveDmPolicy, collectWarnings },
  outbound: {
    base: { deliveryMode, chunker, chunkerMode, textChunkLimit },
    attachedResults: { channel, sendText, sendMedia },
  },
}
```

### 4. **File Size Reference**

**Expected file sizes for similar complexity:**
- `channel.ts` - 200-400 lines
- `client.ts` or `protocol.ts` - 300-500 lines
- `inbound.ts` - 200-400 lines
- `config-schema.ts` - 80-150 lines
- `types.ts` - 100-150 lines
- `accounts.ts` - 150-250 lines
- `setup-surface.ts` - 200-400 lines
- `send.ts` - 50-150 lines
- `normalize.ts` - 100-200 lines
- Other small helpers - 20-80 lines each

---

## SDK IMPORTS BY FUNCTIONALITY

**Core Plugin Creation:**
```typescript
import { createChatChannelPlugin, defineChannelPluginEntry, defineSetupPluginEntry } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core";
```

**Configuration:**
```typescript
import { buildChannelConfigSchema, ... } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-schema";
import { createScopedChannelConfigAdapter, createScopedDmSecurityResolver, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-helpers";
```

**Account Management:**
```typescript
import { createAccountListHelpers, normalizeAccountId, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/account-helpers";
import { resolveMergedAccountConfig } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/account-resolution";
```

**Message Handling:**
```typescript
import { dispatchInboundReplyWithBase, deliverFormattedTextWithAttachments, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc"; // or equivalent
```

**Setup Wizard:**
```typescript
import { createSetupPluginEntry, createPromptParsedAllowFromForAccount, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/setup";
```

**Status/Health:**
```typescript
import { createComputedAccountStatusAdapter, createDefaultChannelRuntimeState, ... } 
  from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/status-helpers";
```

**Utilities:**
```typescript
import { createPluginRuntimeStore } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/runtime-store";
import { runStoppablePassiveMonitor } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/extension-shared";
import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
```

---

## CONFIGURATION PATTERN (package.json openclaw block)

```json
{
  "openclaw": {
    "extensions": ["./index.ts"],
    "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts",
    "channel": {
      "id": "pulseboard",
      "label": "PulseBoard",
      "selectionLabel": "PulseBoard (API Key)",
      "docsPath": "/channels/pulseboard",
      "docsLabel": "pulseboard",
      "blurb": "Real-time messaging via PulseBoard API.",
      "systemImage": "network",
      "quickstartAllowFrom": true
    },
    "install": {
      "minHostVersion": ">=2026.4.1"
    }
  }
}
```

---

## PLUGIN MANIFEST PATTERN (openclaw.plugin.json)

```json
{
  "id": "pulseboard",
  "channels": ["pulseboard"],
  "configSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "additionalProperties": false,
    "properties": {}
  }
}
```

---

This reference covers the complete structure, patterns, and requirements for implementing a channel plugin following the IRC and Nostr templates. Use this to create your PulseBoard plugin with all necessary files and integrations.

15	const IrcGroupSchema = z
16	  .object({
17	    requireMention: z.boolean().optional(),
18	    tools: ToolPolicySchema,
19	    toolsBySender: z.record(z.string(), ToolPolicySchema).optional(),
20	    skills: z.array(z.string()).optional(),

28	  .object({
29	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
30	    service: z.string().optional(),
31	    password: z.string().optional(),
32	    passwordFile: z.string().optional(),
33	    register: z.boolean().optional(),

36	  .strict()

43	      });

Here's what I know:
1. The workspace includes `extensions/*` as plugin packages
2. Each plugin follows this structure:

   - `openclaw.plugin.json` with id, channels, configSchema

   - `setup-entry.ts` - `defineSetupPluginEntry`

5. For PulseBoard specifically, I'm supporting both direct and group chats with a 4000-character text limit, using named boards for group messaging, and the runtime API re-exports from the openclaw plugin SDK.

Now I'm realizing the issue with the current approach - the runtime-api.ts imports from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc`, which is a channel-specific SDK subpath. Since the CLAUDE.md for the plugin SDK says "Do not modify existing files," and adding `openclaw/plugin-sdk/pulseboard.ts` would be a new SDK subpath that would require updating `package.json`, `entrypoints.ts`, etc.

Now I have a very clear picture. The `openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc` subpath already exists (it's `src/plugin-sdk/irc.ts`). For a new PulseBoard channel plugin, since we can't modify existing files, the `runtime-api.ts` would need to import from various publicly exported `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` subpaths directly - like:

- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-pairing` - for `createChannelPairingController`


- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/reply-payload` - for `deliverFormattedTextWithAttachments`
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/status-helpers` - for `buildBaseChannelStatusSummary`

These SDK subpaths are defined as `./plugin-sdk/<name>` in the package exports and map to `src/plugin-sdk/*.ts` files, so I need to verify that each function we need is actually exported from its corresponding public subpath. Let me check what `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-inbound` exports for `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase`.

Looking at `src/plugin-sdk/irc.ts`, which re-exports everything from internal paths, the IRC plugin uses `openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc` as its runtime-api source. For a new PulseBoard plugin, I'd need to find equivalent public subpaths. The irc.ts file exports `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase` from an internal path that maps to `src/plugin-sdk/inbound-reply-dispatch.ts`, but checking the package.json exports, there's no `./plugin-sdk/inbound-reply-dispatch` listed publicly—though there is `./plugin-sdk/channel-inbound`. I need to check what `channel-inbound.ts` actually exports to see if it contains the functions we need.

Since I can't create `src/plugin-sdk/pulseboard.ts`, the PulseBoard `runtime-api.ts` would directly re-export from the publicly available `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` subpaths, similar to how the IRC plugin consolidates exports. I see that `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-inbound` doesn't export `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase`. This is exported from `src/plugin-sdk/inbound-reply-dispatch.ts`, which is NOT a public subpath in the package.json exports. It IS re-exported via `openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc`.

Since the task says "Only create new files in the pulseboard extension directory", we cannot create new SDK subpath files. This means the PulseBoard `runtime-api.ts` needs to think carefully about what it can import from public SDK paths.

But wait - I should approach this differently. 

Looking at how other extensions like `nostr` and `matrix` handle their runtime-api, and comparing with `extensions/irc/src/runtime-api.ts` which imports from the bundled `openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc` facade that's already registered in package.json exports. For PulseBoard, I'm creating a similar bundled plugin, but I can't create `src/plugin-sdk/pulseboard.ts` since that would be outside the extension directory. The constraint here is that I need to work within what's already publicly exported from the SDK.

Checking the package.json exports, I'm not seeing `./plugin-sdk/inbound-reply-dispatch` as a public subpath—it looks like only the channel-specific ones like `irc` and `nostr` are exposed. And interestingly, the Nostr extension doesn't seem to have a `runtime-api.ts` file at all, which suggests it might handle things differently than IRC does.
